data.title: A Tale Of Two Markets: Gold Vs. U.S. Stocks
data.description:
With the PE ratio of the S&P 500 index near all-time highs (particularly relative to real interest rates), the pricing of US stocks is implicitly reflecting exceptional optimism about the prospects for future economic growth, combined with extraordinary complacency about potential macroeconomic risks.

By contrast, with the purchasing power of gold near an all-time high (400+ years), the pricing of gold implicitly reflects exceptional pessimism about long-term macroeconomic conditions and great anxiety about the potential for future inflation.

These drastically contrasting visions about the future that are represented in the price of US equities and gold remind me of two worlds that simultaneously coexisted somewhat uneasily in Charles Dickens' classic novel, A Tale of Two Cities. In this novel, Dickens’ vision of Paris represented a cautionary tale about the latent potential for the collapse of a great and prosperous nation due to internal contradictions. By contrast, Dickens' portrayal of British society represented optimism about the potential for London to avoid the fate of Paris.

Given Dickens' keen knowledge of the deplorable conditions of the lower classes in London at the time of that A Tale of Two Cities was written, one wonders why the author seems to be relatively hopeful about the future of the monarchical and aristocratic society of England, given some very clear parallels that he drew between Victorian Britain and the doomed ancien regime of France. This is a seeming contradiction that is without a ready explanation.

In this article I am going to explore a different, and perhaps even more difficult apparent contradiction: How is it that in efficient capital market, the pricing of one asset can express views about the macroeconomy that fundamentally contradict the views being expressed in the pricing of another asset? Specifically, the question I wish to explore is: How is it possible that in an integrated capital market, the price of gold can reflect a deep pessimism about the future while the price of equities reflects such a radical optimism? Wouldn't arbitrage cause the pricing of different assets to converge toward compatible views?

After we answer these questions, we conclude with a reflection on how unravelling this paradox in financial markets might enable a reader to gain some insight that could explain the apparent contradiction that is at the heart of A Tale of Two Cities.

An Insight into Pricing Theory

The key to understanding the apparent contradiction between the pricing of gold and the pricing of equities lies in the integration of two key insights that are derived from the theory of price formation.

The first insight is that the market price of any asset is not determined by the “average” opinion of market participants with regards to its fair value. It is determined by the opinion of the marginal buyer and seller of that particular asset.

If the pricing of assets were determined by the average opinion of all market participants, then the visions of the macroeconomic future that are implicitly expressed in the prices of gold and US equities would be entirely consistent. But the pricing of assets in financial markets is never determined by average opinion; pricing is determined by the opinions of marginal participants. And, as we shall see, the marginal participants in the markets for US equities and gold are fundamentally different.

The second insight necessary to resolve the apparent contradiction reflected in the prices of gold and US equities is this: All assets are always owned by the highest bidders. Stated differently, assets are always owned by the most optimistic fringe (regarding the prospects for that asset) among all market participants. This means that the pricing of any asset always reflects the opinions of a narrow fringe of the most optimistic market participants with regards to that asset.

Now, let’s apply this insight to the pricing of gold and US equities.

The Price of Equities is Determined by Optimists

In accordance with the fundamental microeconomic principle just described above, every single stock that exists is always owned by the market participants that value it most highly. This means that the valuation of the S&P 500 on aggregate, always reflects the most wildly optimistic valuations of each individual stock that comprises the index. The end result is that, on aggregate, the valuation of an index such as the S&P 500 will generally reflect a valuation that most market participants will deem to be unreasonably high. This “unreasonably high” valuation paradoxically projects an optimistic opinion about the future regarding aggregate growth and profitability of US companies that very few of the shareholders of the companies that comprise the index actually share.

In other words, if you polled the shareholders of individual companies that comprise the S&P 500 index regarding their opinions regarding broad economic conditions and broader profitability trends, they would overwhelmingly tend to have opinions that are less optimistic than the valuation of the overall S&P 500 index projects.

This creates a paradox where the market valuation of the aggregate index does not accurately represent the macroeconomic views of the shareholders of the individual companies that comprise the index. The macro opinion projected by the valuation of the index is always far more optimistic than the opinions of the average participant and/or the average of opinions of the participants.

But it is crucial to understand a critical distinction: The high valuation of an index such as the S&P 500 is not a function of the fact that the owners of equities are optimistic about the economy or the overall market. They are only optimistic about the specific stocks that they own. But since the price of each stock is always determined by the most optimistic market participants, the aggregate value of the overall market will always project an implied optimism that far exceeds the actual assessments of the majority of market participants.

The Price of Gold Is Determined by Pessimists

The extremely optimistic opinion regarding the future of the macroeconomy that is projected in the aggregate price of equities has a mirror-image counterpart in the pricing of gold. The pricing of gold projects the most optimistic opinions about gold. But the most optimistic opinions about gold are driven by those that are most pessimistic about the future of the macroeconomy and the stock market. People that are reasonably optimistic about the future do not tend to be marginal buyers of gold. The marginal buyers of gold are always those that are so pessimistic about the future that they are willing to outbid all potential buyers in the market.

Therefore, the price of gold always reflects the opinions of the most pessimistic fringe among market participants.

What is Different Now?

Everything about the pricing of equities and gold which I described above has always true and will always be true. These basic facts have never changed and will never change. These insights are derived from fundamental microeconomic principles. So, what is different now? What enables us to explain the particularly extraordinary divergence of opinions that are currently projected by the pricing of equities and gold?

The difference is this: The optimistic fringe of market participants has almost never been as optimistic as they are now, and the pessimistic fringe has almost never been as pessimistic as they are now. There has been a polarization and a radicalization of opinion at the fringes of the market. Therefore, when you put these two facts together with the insights about price formation that I have laid out, it becomes apparent why the divergence in the opinions reflected in gold pricing and equity pricing are greater than they have ever been.

Why Is There Such a Radical Difference of Opinion?

It is important to remember that, in trying to reconcile the opinions implicitly projected in the pricing of equities and gold, it makes virtually no difference what the opinions are of average market participants. In the pricing of assets that trade in financial markets, the opinions of the marginal participants are the only ones that are relevant.

In this essay, I cannot go into depth about all of the reasons for the polarization and radicalization of opinions at the margins of financial markets. However, I can provide a general outline of an answer that should suffice.

The first reason that I can point to is related to the polarization of political and/or ideologically-based opinions about the future of the US economy. This is a well-documented development in the political science literature. It is, therefore, not surprising to see this polarization and radicalization of opinions about the future of the US economy at the fringes among financial markets participants.

Second, I believe that the polarization that is observed in opinions about the future of the US economy can be explained in large part due to the highly polarized opinions on two specific topics: 1) Artificial intelligence. 2) National debt.

Regarding artificial intelligence, the pricing of US equities is becoming increasingly dominated by the opinions of the most optimistic fringe of market participants on the topic of AI. While there are many skeptics and even pessimists regarding AI – indeed, they may even constitute a majority of market participants -- their opinions are irrelevant to market pricing of individual stocks. The pricing of every US stock is ultimately dominated by the most optimistic market participants. And since owners of most US stocks are almost necessarily the most optimistic about what AI will do for the growth and profitability of the stocks they own, then it will tend to be their very optimistic views of the impact of AI on their own individual stocks that will be projected in the aggregate valuation of the market.

Regarding the national debt, this issue hardly registers at all among the most optimistic equity investors. In other words, if you ask the owners of individual stocks (who are necessarily the most optimistic market participants regarding that stock), it is likely that most of them will say that the national debt has played no role in their valuation of that stock. By contrast, if you ask the marginal investor of gold what factors led them to buy gold, they will probably cite the US national debt as one of the top reasons (if not the single most important reason). Therefore, as the national debt has become an increasingly pressing issue, it is no surprise that pessimism on this issue has become visibly reflected in the price of gold.

Paradox Solved

In sum, there is no fundamental economic contradiction reflected in the pricing of equities and gold. The paradox dissolves when we realize that the marginal buyer of gold is more pessimistic than ever about the factors that influence the pricing of gold (e.g. national debt) and the marginal buyer of equities is more optimistic than ever about the factors that tend to drive the valuation of equities (e.g. impact of artificial intelligence).

Interpreting Dickens’ Tale of Two Cities in Light of Our Tale of Two Assets

It is obviously beyond the scope of this article to provide a full interpretation of A Tale of Two Cities. However, I think we can apply one insight explored in this article to the analysis of the situation affecting pre-revolutionary France and England in the novel – and Victorian England at the time that the novel was published. Specifically, we want to know: What explains the relative optimism that is implicit in the novel regarding the future of England in light of the events of revolutionary France? We think that this apparent contradiction can, in part, be explained as follows:

In France, the French citizens that were most pessimistic about their own future wanted revenge and a violent revolution, even if it was at the cost of their own lives. This is a functional equivalent of the most pessimistic market participants demanding gold and driving its price up to levels that project a crisis.

In Britain, even the Englishmen that were most pessimistic about the future of their own country dreamed that they themselves might one day marry, have children and perhaps buy shares of stock on the London Stock Exchange. This is the functional equivalent of many investors that might be somewhat pessimistic about the economic future of the USA, but are reasonably optimistic about the future of the stocks that they own.

A more fundamental difference between the "marginal participants" of two societies can scarcely be imagined. In my view, it is fundamentally this difference of mindset among the “marginal participants” of the societies of pre-revolutionary France and England which ultimately told the "tale of two cities."

Application to Current Market Outlook

The application of the principles illustrated in this essay to the current market outlook are somewhat evident, upon reflection.

First, despite the extremely widespread belief that the overall equity market is “overvalued,” we can predict that its value will not decline significantly unless and until the most optimistic fringe among market participants become more pessimistic than they are currently. In practice, this would require a shift in the mindset of this most optimistic cohort of market participants. Specifically, we believe that fears of a cyclical recession would need to begin to (at least temporarily) over-ride the long-term optimism about AI that has taken hold of their imaginations.

Regarding the outlook for gold, it is quite evident that the most pessimistic fringe among market participants has become convinced that the US is on the verge of a debt crisis. However, it is a characteristic of human nature that the intensity of pessimism about the future is rarely maintained for very long. If and when the immediacy and/or urgency of this fear wears off (at least temporarily), the marginal demand for gold will wane -- particularly in light of the extremely high price of gold relative to virtually all other goods and services. A decline in the price of gold does not require that the flow of bad news about debts and deficits diminish. All that is required is that the flow of bad news remain of the same nature and at roughly the same rate.

It is our view that changes in the mindsets of marginal participants in both gold and equity markets will occur at some point during the remainder of 2025. We expect these changes to cause a significant downward adjustment in the prices of US equities and gold – although not necessarily at the same exact points in time.

Final Thoughts

At Successful Portfolio Strategy, we have a unique fundamental understanding of how the pricing of assets work. The pricing of assets does not only depend on a correct interpretation of fundamentals. It also depends on a correct interpretation of who the marginal buyers and sellers are and what their behaviors are likely to be given the fundamental environment. By having a unique understanding of both fundamentals and key asset pricing principles, we are able to identify opportunities that almost everybody else misses. For example, in late 2024, we were able to predict that both US equities and gold would likely rise in price, despite generally being uncorrelated, due to the fact that the election of Donald Trump would likely increase marginal demand for equities and gold. We also anticipated that equities would likely rise despite a likely increase in Treasury yields – despite the fact that consensus of opinion would typically predict the opposite. Our reasoning was that the marginal investor in bonds tends to be sensitive to inflation risk and increasing debt in a way that the marginal equity investor is not. This manner of analysis also proved to be the key to understanding why gold would rise despite rising bond yields (historical correlations of treasury yields and gold tend to be highly negative). In this case, our insight was that higher bond yields would be offset by an explosion of pessimism among marginal investors about debt and inflation.

Fundamentals matter. But they act through the medium of the opinions and behavior of marginal buyers and sellers.

data.title: TriplePoint Venture Growth: 18% Dividend Yield Is A Red Flag (Downgrade)
data.description:
TriplePoint Venture Growth BDC (NYSE:TPVG) slashed its dividend in the third quarter of 2024 and since then, the business development company has exhibited softening dividend payout metrics, which could put even the lower dividend at risk again of a second cut.

TriplePoint Venture Growth BDC has consistently underperformed in terms of its non-accruals and has racked up losses from its investment portfolio in the last year on a near continuous basis.

Unfortunately, my previous optimism about TriplePoint Venture Growth BDC has proven to have been unfounded, and I think that a lower stock classification 'Hold' is appropriate taking the heightened investment risks.

My last stock classification on TriplePoint Venture Growth BDC was 'Buy', primarily because the business development company experienced a major valuation haircut that was directly related to its high degree of investment losses that crashed the company's net assets and net investment income.

The non-accrual ratio is not improving as much as I had hoped and with the margin of dividend safety narrowing again in the last two quarters, I think that another dividend may be on the horizon for TriplePoint Venture Growth BDC.

Non-Accruals And Investment Losses Reflect Investment Risks

TriplePoint Venture Growth is offering capital to venture-backed technology companies and therefore has considerable overlap, in its strategic direction, with other business development companies like Hercules Capital Inc. (HTGC) and Horizon Technology Finance (HRZN) both of which serve the same market segment of middle-market companies.

TriplePoint Venture Growth owns primarily debt investments, a total of $565.4 million to be precise, which is supplemented by equity and warrant investments that had a total value of $116.6 million.

Equity and warrants therefore account for a whopping 17.1% of the business development company's investment portfolio as of March 31, 2025, making the BDC reliant on strong equity performance moving forward.

TriplePoint Venture Growth does not have the best investment record, and it is showing in the BDC's net asset value, which has decreased a whopping 38% since Q4 2021, predominantly because its debt losses have surged. In the last five quarters, the business development company has seen a steady stream of troubled loans that hurt TriplePoint Venture Growth's net asset value as well as net investment income performance.

Since Q1 2024, TriplePoint Venture Growth has produced $4.1 million in net realized and unrealized losses each quarter, which is mainly due to an underperforming credit portfolio, characterized by an above average level of non-accruals.

High losses, both realized and unrealized, have taken a substantial bite out of the BDC's net asset value in recent years, leading to a peer group-leading discount to net asset value.

TriplePoint Venture Growth BDC has been plagued by above average non-accruals and investment losses that have seriously dented the business development company's performance, particularly last year.

As of March 31, 2025, TriplePoint Venture Growth BDC had a non-accrual ratio of 3.6% (shown in the chart below as a % of the debt investment portfolio at fair value) which was down 0.1 percentage points QoQ.

Though the performance trend in impaired loans shows signs of improvement, as non-accruals appear to have peaked in Q2 2024 at 8.2%, the business development company still has to deal with a higher amount of impaired loans than the average BDC.

Hercules Capital, for instance, had a non-accrual ratio of only 0.5% in the March-quarter and therefore offers passive income investors a substantially enhanced credit profile.

Payout Metrics

TriplePoint Venture Growth BDC slashed its dividend by 25% to $0.30 per share in the third quarter of 2024 amid a surge in investment losses and a simultaneous decrease in net investment income.

In Q1 2025, the business development company earned only $0.27 in net investment income from its debt investments, thus falling $0.03 short of the $0.30 per share per quarter dividend in the payout ratio.

Only three quarters after the BDC adjusted its dividend lower, TriplePoint Venture Growth BDC's dividend payout ratio again rose above 100% in Q1 2025.

Hercules Capital and Horizon Technology Finance are two other business development companies that have the same market strategy and serve the same customers. Hercules Capital, on an LTM basis, had the best dividend payout ratio of 81% while Horizon Technology Finance could also be staring down a dividend cut with an LTM payout of 108%.

Serious Discount To NAV Reflects Heightened Dividend Risk

Ultra-high dividend yields in the BDC industry very rarely last for a long time, as they are more often than not are a reflection of underlying credit and dividend coverage problems. In the case of TPVG, the stock presently yields 18%, a clear red flag if you asked me.

TriplePoint Venture Growth BDC is selling for the highest discount to net asset value for reasons mentioned above. Presently, this discount sits at 22%, reflecting the risk of an imminent dividend cut.

Horizon Technology Finance, whose dividend is not the safest either, is selling for 0.99x net asset value, whereas Hercules Capital is getting the only premium in the BDC peer group with a Price-To-NAV ratio of 1.49x.

In my view, the present discount to TPVG's net asset value properly reflects the risk of a second dividend cut.

Hercules Capital, however, has earned a reputation of being extremely meticulous and diligent in its loan originations and the dividend is regularly covered well by the BDC's net investment income.

Not surprisingly, Hercules Capital also has the highest Price-To-NII ratio of 9.7x whereas both TriplePoint Venture Growth BDC and Horizon Technology Finance sell for substantially less, 6-7x NII.

Why TPVG Might Sell For An Even Bigger NAV Discount

Business development companies have gotten clobbered in the last two months, though we have seen valuation recoveries, particularly for BDCs that have strong credit histories and good dividend coverage. Despite market-driven volatility, there are riskier and less risky choices for BDCs as investment vehicles, and TPVG is definitely a higher-risk BDC.

In my view, TriplePoint Venture Growth BDC has considerable dividend adjustment risk in the short term, which is why I am lowering my stock classification back to 'Hold.'

If TPVG indeed slashed its dividend a second time in a short period of time, I think that the stock could be in for more pain and possibly sell for a much bigger discount to net asset value.

My Takeaway

TriplePoint Venture Growth BDC is a struggling business development company as the BDC's dividend payout metrics continued to soften in the last two quarters. This is highly unfortunate, taking into account that TPVG already slashed its dividend last year by 25% in order to realign its dividend payout with lower net investment income.

The business development company still has a very high non-accrual ratio of 3.6% which is unacceptable, particularly with BDC like Hercules Capital providing a stark contrast in terms of credit quality.

The dividend has not been covered by net investment income in Q1 2025, and the margin of safety has complete vanished, suggesting that TriplePoint Venture Growth BDC might have to announce a second dividend cut. Hold.

data.title: Teleperformance: Supportive Momentum Ongoing And AI Upside
data.description:
Following the Q1 release of Teleperformance (OTCPK:TLPFF) (OTCPK:TLPFY), we are back to comment on the market leader in the outsourced customer experience. Our buy rating was supported by 1) a more Specialized Services company with higher EBITDA evolution 2) AI Upside To Price In, 3) market mispriced of Majorel's latest acquisition, underestimating its strategic value and long-term earnings potential and 4) >+14% free cash flow yield with downside protection due to an ongoing buyback and a >4% dividend yield. Since our last coverage (Q4 and FY 2024 results), Teleperformance's share price has remained flat, but the company has paid its dividend (Fig. 1), and the total return has reached a plus 5%.

Fig 1

Q1 Results and Our Positive Take

The company reported solid first-quarter results, with sales growth of +1.6% year-on-year to €2.6 billion. Cross-checking Wall Street consensus estimates, Teleperformance beat the average analyst's expectation, which was forecasting a +1.1% growth. This modest growth beat was led by encouraging trends in Core Services, but from a segment perspective, Specialised Services grew by 10.7% (Fig. 2). Despite a volatile operating environment and some known contract headwinds (TLS), the company managed to achieve solid sales momentum in Europe and the Middle East. Additionally, the sales growth in the Specialized Services division was supported by ZP integration. As a reminder, in Q1, the company did not disclose its margin and earnings; however, Teleperformance has reaffirmed its Fiscal Year 2025 guidance for +2-4% LFL growth, a group EBITA margin of 15.0-15.1% (Fig. 4), and a free cash flow of at least €1 billion.

Source: Teleperformance Q1 results ppt - Fig 2

Why are we still Positive?

  1. While much of the industry is focused on the growing role of AI in customer relationship management, there are essential counterexamples, most notably Klarna. Here at the Lab, we already commented on that. Interestingly, Klarna was one of the companies that triggered a sell-off in Teleperformance shares following news that Klarna would increasingly use AI to handle customer interactions. However, Klarna has changed course. The CEO recently made it clear that customers will always have the option to speak with a real person, and the company now plans to bring in more staff to strengthen its support team. This reflects how, for some customers, speaking with a human still matters a great deal. This is a positive upside for Teleperformance and cannot go unnoticed;
  2. Given the limited company's Q1 disclosure, we see support in the unchanged 2025 guidance, especially with the support of Specialized Services sales. It appears that Teleperformance is effectively integrating ZP. The company specializes in communication services tailored to the deaf and hard-of-hearing community (Fig. 3) —a niche market that smaller competitors may lack the resources or scale to serve effectively. Still related to M&A, Majorel integration is progressing well, and Teleperformance also reaffirmed its cost synergy target of €20-€30 million for the year. This comes in addition to the €94 million in synergies already realized in 2024. There still appears to be additional potential to extract further efficiencies as the integration continues;
  3. During the quarter, it entered into partnerships with two leading Agentic AI companies — Ema and Parloa — as part of its broader investment program, which is set to allocate up to €100 million in 2025. Beyond enhancing operational efficiency in addressing client needs, these collaborations could provide a competitive edge over rivals that have yet to adopt similar technologies. Furthermore, should these partnerships prove successful, Teleperformance retains the option to participate in the next funding rounds for both firms. These deals follow an earlier partnership with Sanas. The company is leveraging Sanas technology to neutralize the accents of customer service representatives based in India and the Philippines, aiming to enhance communication clarity and improve the customer experience. We expect the economics of this and itsimplications to be a focus at the upcoming CMD on 18th June;
  4. With the support of GEO sales, Teleperformance expanded its presence in the Euro area. Growth was well balanced across GEO, and management remains confident in an acceleration over the year.

Fig 3

Fig 4

Earnings Changes and Valuation

Last time, we reported a disruption in TLS Visa application sales, and we continue to forecast top-line sales of €10.8 billion. This is aligned with management guidance. Following the Q1 print, we should forecast higher growth in the Specialized Services segment. At this stage, we prefer to remain prudent and await further evidence at the upcoming Capital Market Day. B2B service spending is likely to come under some form of pressure, and there is also an adverse FX evolution to be priced in. That said, Teleperformance has much upside potential on ZP consolidation (which is already delivering) and Majorel cost synergies. We continue to believe the top management team is broadly cautious, especially on EBITA expansion. Continuing to apply a 15.1% margin, we reached €1.63 billion EBITA. With the new financing debt, we estimate an interest payment of at least €200 million per year; however, the company has €1 billion FCF potential for deleverage per year. In our base-case scenario, we continue to apply a €500 million buyback and a €500 million debt reduction. Applying a tax rate of 25.5% aligned with Teleperformance's historical average, we project a net profit forecast of €1.03 billion. Considering the current share numbers, Teleperformance EPS reached €17.2. This still does not assume the ongoing buyback.

At this stage, Teleperformance trades at an EV/EBITDA ratio of less than 5x and a P/E ratio of 5.23x. The company's current valuation remains attractive, even without considering the potential margin upside. We are somewhat surprised that the stock did not react more positively following the encouraging Klarna update, particularly considering how it has responded sharply to negative news in the past year. This is also well below Teleperformance's historical average (Figs. 5 and 6). With a double-digit free cash flow yield, for consistency reasons, we confirmed our 13x price-to-earnings target. Following a minor EPS evolution, our valuation is set at €223.60 per share ($126 in ADR). Thus, our Strong Buy Recommendation is sustained.

Fig 5

Fig 6

Risks and Threats

We have extensive coverage of Teleperformance, so we report the following downside risks: 1) decline in Specialized Services sales following the non-renewal of the TLScontact contract highlights a potential risk for the company; 2) new technology and incumbent players, 3) challenging pricing environment, 4) continued CAPEX investing and failure to adopt and effectively integrate emerging technologies, 5) client concentration risk, 6) wage inflation and FX volatility, 7) regulatory risks, for instance, in 2003, the company faced strict USA regulations regarding the "Do Not Call List," and 8) negative investor sentiment toward the sector.

Regarding a new risk, we report that the company has recently undertaken restructuring initiatives in both Spain and France for a total consideration of €54 million. This is already included in our estimates, but it may signal that the company is facing certain operational headwinds.

Conclusion

There appear to be two opposing views on GenAI’s impact on the customer experience industry: one suggesting it undermines the very foundation of the CX business model, and another viewing it as a new tool that companies in the sector will integrate, making the recent derating unjustified. We align with the latter perspective. In our view, the company is well-positioned to offer a hybrid solution, and with a compelling valuation discount, we reiterate our investment conviction.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Cerrado Gold Inc. 2025 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Cerrado Gold Inc. in conjunction with their 2025 Q1 earnings call.

data.title: Prices Re-Tethering To Income
data.description:
Ultra-luxury goods are often regarded as recession-proof, with steady demand regardless of the state of the economy. But that’s not what is happening.

During the pandemic, when ‘free money’ policies sent demand for goods through the roof, many luxury companies seized the opportunity, raising prices by an average of 36% between 2020 and 2023, according to Bernstein analysis (chart below)—roughly double the rate of overall U.S. inflation at the time. For a while, demand proved relatively inelastic, holding up despite soaring prices.

This was good for companies, where higher prices with steady demand increased total revenue. Now, as with unaffordable homes, cars and motor homes, ‘greedflation’ is coming back to bite in the form of weaker demand.

With excess inventory building in many sectors, cheaper options are taking market share from rivals that consumers now consider overpriced. That’s a problem for those who cannot or will not reduce prices, and then there is the matter of who will pay for new tariffs. See Luxury Brands Are Paying for Over-the-Top Price Hikes:

Luxury brands also can’t cut prices without damaging their image. They spend billions of dollars a year on advertising to convince shoppers to pay steep markups on their goods. Charging less is an admission that they have misread the market and that their goods aren’t as desirable as thought.

More likely, labels will sit on their hands until inflation and income growth slowly make their goods seem more affordable. They can also design new lower-priced products to boost sales.

Brands that showed restraint during the pandemic have the best chance of shielding their profit margins from President Trump’s tariffs.

…At the end of the day everything has its price, even luxury.

Disclosure: No positions.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Fairfax Holdings: Misunderstood Conglomerate With Some Upside
data.description:
Introduction

Fairfax Holdings Inc. (TSX:FFH:CA) is a cautious buy today given their diverse asset mix, growing core insurance business, and undervaluation from a peers perspective. FFH:CA is a Canadian insurance and financial services company, with operations globally. The company's core competencies include property and casualty insurance, reinsurance, and investments. FFH:CA had a book value of $1,060 (all figures in CAD unless otherwise noted) as of Dec. 31, 2024 and includes business segments divided by industry, notably insurance, investments, and other. Given the earnings split on both insurance and investments are similar, I think investors are cautious and don't quite understand the business, hence the discount in book value per share.

FFH:CA has had a strong past 12 months, rising over 50%, and I think some gains can continue in this volatile trading environment. While the bond market has seen yields rise over the past couple months, I think FFH:CA can continue to outperform from an investing standpoint. As long as their insurance business continues to grow even close to the 10%+ which we saw in 2024, I think FFH:CA can close the book value per share gap with peers, towards 2x by end of 2025. With a base case book value per share (BVPS) of $1,250 in 2025, in line with their estimate of 15% growth, I can see the stock bumping up to $2,500 before the year end.

In Canada, the company operates mostly through its property and casualty insurance subsidiaries, providing coverage for both personal and commercial clients. Meanwhile, in the United States, FFH:CA has a more diverse insurance presence, offering a range of specialty insurance products through multiple subsidiaries. The firm's international operations focus on general insurance solutions tailored to local markets, with selective involvement in reinsurance and specialty lines, but it depends on the country. Outside of insurance, FFH:CA manages a significant investment portfolio, with investments in public equities, fixed income, and private businesses across sectors. Unlike traditional insurers, the company combines underwriting operations with investment-driven returns, making it a unique hybrid-conglomerate not too dissimilar from Warren Buffett's company. The company has continued to drive strong performance while operating in a hybrid-conglomerate type structure similar to Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B).

Industry and Competitive Analysis

FFH:CA has diversified revenue streams, including global property and casualty insurance, reinsurance, and investment operations, that position the company well to capitalize on a higher interest rate environment. Unlike life insurers, FFH:CA benefits more directly from higher rates through improved yields on its fixed income portfolio and wider underwriting margins. Historically, higher 10-year bond yields have expanded net investment income across Canadian insurers, and FFH:CA is well-positioned given its longer-duration bond portfolio and equity investments. Around 70% of the company's income over the last several years has been boosted by investment performance. Coupled with good underwriting growth amidst their global footprint, FFH:CA stands to benefit both from core insurance profitability and from improved returns on invested assets in the current macroeconomic climate.

The company's key public competitors include Intact Financial (IFC:CA) and Definity Financial (DFY:CA), who just acquired the Canadian operations of Travellers Insurance (TRV) for a book value of 1.4x, which I think will lead to meaningful synergies for DFY:CA. Additionally, I add BRK.B as a competitor, given its similar conglomerate structure, with significant P&C insurance exposure, though noting its scale is over 20x FFH:CA.

As you can see from a peers comparison, FFH:CA is undervalued by almost every metric - but that said, they need to take some responsibility. While the business has continued to operate at a healthy growth clip, with both book value and share price growing at a 17%+ annual clip over four decades, the business is simply too difficult to understand. With both insurance and private equity holdings globally, investors are weary to give the company the same multiple as their pure play P&C peers given the earnings certainty risk, and therefore the company has outperformed while still being undervalued.

Recent Q1 Results

Risk Profile

The company's operations protect it from some single-market shocks, but two key risks remain top of mind. The first is catastrophe exposure, especially given the climate crisis is continuing and evolving. The Los Angeles wildfires this past quarter underscore the vulnerability of the business as a whole. The loss, though within guidance, but at the high end, clipped the consolidated combined ratio. And although these losses are usually looked at as non-recurring, global climate shocks continue to happen more often and could lead to profit declines from reinsurance partners and pressure underwriting margins if they persist.

The other large risk I see in FFH:CA is valuation volatility. While the company has made some savvy long-term investments, I think concentrated bets in select names will always lead the company have a smaller multiple. Another thing management talked about in the earnings call was the potential Boat Rocker/Blue Ant media IPO - while likely value accretive, investors don't always get the different equity moves, and there's a reason conglomerates have declined over the last few decades. Equity and investment exposure lead to book value sensitivity from market swings... that said, the company remains disciplined in capital allocation and continues to reward long-term holders through consistent share buybacks and solid long-term outperformance.

Fundamentals Show Upside

The forecasted target share price was modeled using price to book, given the complexity of its various operating segments as a conglomerate. Historically, FFH:CA has had a P/E of ~10, slightly below its peers given its large scale and confusing operations. That said though, according to LSEG, the company is better scored on valuation than its peers and is trading at a discount to its trailing P/E.

I also agree with a lot of this author's sum of the parts valuation from a recent article on FFH:CA. He notes that the stock is trading on the low end of an estimated range of 1.4x-2.2x P/B and I don't disagree. While FFH:CA will never get a pure-play multiple of 2+ P/B like their Canadian P&C peers, I think that by continued improving growth in the underlying business, along with clear reporting of wins in global equity, they can close the gap as uncertainty persists.

Conclusion

FFH:CA is a cautious buy today, and I expect more strong book value growth, share buybacks, and good investments. Given the leadership's proven execution globally and well-capitalized structure, FFH:CA should succeed in the latter half of the year and see the multiple expand slightly. I think that while the stock won't repeat its fantastic 2024, it should trend toward $2,500 by the end of the year.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Viking Holdings: FY26 Bookings Are Trending Well, But I Am Worried About Pricing
data.description:
Investment overview

I downgraded Viking Holdings (NYSE:VIK) from buy to hold previously as I was worried about the demand situation, especially given the change in competitive dynamics. With 1Q25 results now out, I think some of my concerns have eased. FY26 booking volume is stronger than expected, and the FY25 outlook is largely de-risked. That said, initial FY26 pricing trends are still soft, and until net yield growth reaccelerates, I think it’s too early to turn bullish.

1Q25 Earnings: A Strong Performance

VIK kicked off 1Q25 with a strong set of numbers. Revenue grew 24.9% y/y to $897.1 million, driven by a 14.9% increase in capacity (available passenger cruise days [APCD]) and a 7.1% rise in net yield. By segment, Ocean carried net yield was up 13.6% y/y, helped by an easy comp (only one world cruise this year vs. two last year) and a healthy pricing backdrop, while River saw a 2.7% decline in yield, which should not be a focus since 1Q25 is just 6% of River’s annual PCDs, and the main sailing season runs from April to October. Total adj. EBITDA came in at $72.8 million, and this shows that the business has good operating leverage since the larger revenue base (~3x higher than 1Q19) translated to substantially better adj. EBITDA margins (was negative in FY19, but now 8.1%).

FY25 and FY26 Outlook: Significantly De-Risked

The first piece of good news here is that FY25 consensus numbers are largely de-risked. Back in my last update, VIK had already sold 88% of its core capacity. As of 1Q25 earnings, that figure has climbed to 92%, which management rightly described as “practically sold out.” Notably, pricing has stayed firm. Advance bookings per PCD are up about 7% y/y, with River pricing seeing a notable jump of 6.7% y/y (vs. 5.3% in February), and Ocean pricing held steady at 8.4% y/y. From an equity investor perspective, it removes a huge chunk of uncertainty around FY25 financials.

The second piece of good news is that FY26 bookings are off to a much stronger start than I expected. One of the bigger question marks in my previous update was the lack of visibility beyond FY25, which made it hard to assess how sustainable the demand environment really was. That concern has now been eased. Per the 1Q25 update, VIK has already sold 37% of its core product capacity for 2026. What makes that even more impressive is that 2026 capacity is set to grow 8% y/y. To put it simply, VIK managed to secure a large chunk of business early on top of a large selling capacity, and remember, this is for sailings that are still over a year away. Just on this booking metric, it turned me to be more bullish on the demand backdrop. Management also pointed to clear momentum in recent weeks. Bookings in April and May were both up y/y, with May specifically flagged as having an “outstanding start” and “good strength” in the last few weeks. This 1Q25 update really clears the doubt I had previously, and in my view, heavily improved the growth outlook.

FY26 Pricing

That said, I wouldn’t call the FY26 outlook fully de-risked just yet. Things have definitely improved, but I am still in the “cautious” camp. While booking volumes are strong, pricing is the one that I am a little puzzled about. Advance bookings per PCD are currently tracking at 4% y/y for FY26, which is not bad, but this is a significantly softer start than the 12% y/y that FY25 saw at the same point last year. This got me thinking if the reason volume bookings were strong was because of softer pricing gains. If this is true, then it begs the question of whether VIK can continue to print its usual ~7% net yield growth it did over the past three years. A lower net yield growth would make the growth trajectory much less attractive.

That said, I wouldn’t rush to call this a bearish red flag either. I think management's explanation has some merits. They noted FY25’s early pricing curve had a few one-off events that did not repeat this year. For instance, there are fewer low-yielding world cruises in 1Q25, and there was a heavy skew of early bookings toward high-yield Egypt sailings. FY26 doesn’t have those same tailwinds. Given that VIK’s management team did deliver 7% net yield growth for the past few years, I am giving them the benefit of the doubt that their explanation is the cause, but this is something I will monitor and only change my view when they report tangible numbers in the P&L.

Fleet expansion

Suppose all things check out (i.e., FY26 pricing and bookings stay healthy). I am looking forward to turning bullish as the underlying drivers for growth stay intact: Consumers demand, as I noted above, and fleet growth. On the latter, the pace of expansion is on track. VIK recently confirmed two new ocean ships for delivery in 2031, with options for two more in 2033. It’s also expanding on the river side, with a new vessel for Portugal set to launch in 2027. One area that I believe is interesting, though the impact is still very early, is the Viking Libra, coming in 2026, which will be the world’s first hydrogen-powered cruise ship. While the impact won’t be immediate, I see this as a smart long-term move. It lowers reliance on traditional fuels, and if this tech scales well across the fleet, it could offer more cost predictability (fuel prices are volatile and depend on the macro situation) and potentially improve margins over time (when hydrogen costs reduce over time). For now, the near-term benefit is small (just a few more cabins from layout adjustments), but I think the direction is positive.

The current ship order book also supports a steady runway for capacity growth. VIK plans to bring on 11 new ocean ships by 2031 and 27 new river ships by 2029. If the demand environment holds and pricing (net yield growth) remains steady, this expansion should translate directly into solid adj. EBITDA growth.

Valuation

In my March update, I mentioned that VIK deserves to trade at 12x forward EBITDA because the demand outlook has become uncertain, with signs of potential slowdown. This appears not to be the case—so far, from the FY26 booking numbers—but I am not fully convinced about the strength behind this yet (because of the slower net yield growth). However, suppose VIK is able to show the market that FY26 bookings and pricing remain healthy; I see a good chance for VIK multiples to see an upgrade. Currently, the market values VIK at 12.5x forward EBITDA, pretty much in line with Royal Caribbean Cruises (RCL). This relative gap should be wider if VIK can show revenue growth sustained at mid-teens (8% FY26 capacity growth + historical 7% net yields growth) vs. RCL’s expected growth of ~10%. The near-term upgrade is probably 13.5x forward EBITDA, the level VIK traded just two weeks ago before the earnings. At 13.5x, I have a price target of $55.

But I am not going to upgrade to a buy rating today. I would wait for 1 or 2 more quarters of data about FY26 to confirm that net yield growth can track back to 7% or higher and bookings strength did not soften.

Conclusion

I reiterate a hold rating for VIK. 1Q25 gave a clearer line of sight into FY25 and FY26, but I’m not ready to upgrade until I see proof that net yield growth can recover to ~7% and that FY26 booking cycle strength stays strong. If that happens, VIK has a good shot at multiple expansions, given its strong brand, forward booking momentum, and fleet growth strategy. For now, I think the stock trades fairly, and I’ll stay on the sidelines while watching upcoming data closely.

data.title: Beam Therapeutics: A Cautious Hold On A Base Editing Pioneer
data.description:
Introduction

I last covered Beam Therapeutics (NASDAQ:BEAM) in October 2023. My first instinct was to glance at its pipeline to see if the gene therapy developer had made any progress. Recall that the company was experiencing some delays in patient recruitment in its BEACON Phase 1/2 trial evaluating BEAM-101 in sickle cell disease, or SCD. According to the NIH site, the study is still “recruiting” patients. I have suspicions that these delays may be because there are two FDA-approved gene therapies already available for SCD, and neither product is “flying off the shelves.” So, if this is any indication of how things are going for Beam, it is not good. Its stock echoes this sentiment. It’s down almost 25% since my Sell rating versus S&P 500 gains of 37% in the same period.

In the following article, I discovered that not much has changed with Beam, warranting continued caution.

Recent Developments

The difference between Beam’s pipeline then and now is that its CAR-T investigational therapies are missing.

Beam deemphasized its CAR-T aspirations just days after my October 2023 evaluation. While this reduces optionality, it was probably necessary to curb its high cash burn. Plus, I didn’t believe Beam’s prospects in CAR-T were good, considering there are a few marketed and established CAR-T products.

BEAM-101 remains in Phase 1/2 development, and the company plans to present data during EHA in June.

Beam has a couple of therapies targeting rarer diseases than SCD, like alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency (AATD) and glycogen storage disease type 1a (GSD1A). In my view, these markets have lower barriers to entry than SCD.

For AATD, it may be challenging to get patients on augmentation therapy to roll the dice with a one-and-done gene therapy. I imagine similar market dynamics to SCD. Moreover, there are several potentially disease-modifying drugs in development for AATD, including other gene therapies. So, Beam’s therapy will likely have to be differentiated from other treatments to succeed in this market.

In March, Beam unveiled data after just 28 days in nine AATD patients. After a single infusion of BEAM-302, patients saw improvements in total and functional AAT across all three dose levels. From my porch, the data is a bit challenging to interpret, but this is not unexpected given its immaturity.

Moving on, GSD1A patients don’t have many options outside of a strict dietary treatment. The goal here is to prevent episodes of low blood sugar, which could be life-threatening if not treated promptly. So, again, this is a niche market and unlikely to be a simple launch. Beam recently dosed the first patient in a Phase ½ clinical trial that evaluates BEAM-301 in GSD1A.

Should BEAM-101 underwhelm, the company does have some optionality in sickle cell disease with BEAM-103, BEAM-104, and in vivo HSC editing. It is also targeting beta-thalassemia, but like SCD, this already has an approved gene therapy.

Collaborations with Pfizer (PFE) and Apellis (APLS) help validate its technology, but don’t offer much financial relief in the short term. These deals are inherently backloaded, although the 2022 Pfizer collaboration did involve $300 million upfront (with $1 billion in potential milestone payments).

Turning to Beam’s financials, it did a common stock offering this March, raising around $500 million. Following the raise, Beam’s cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities totaled $1.22 billion, which the company estimates is sufficient to fund operations “into 2028.”

Without significant revenue generation, the raise, at the expense of shareholder dilution, was necessary to fund Beam’s exuberant R&D expenditures. In the first quarter of 2025, R&D expenses were $98.8 million. G&A expenses were $27.9 million. Beam’s net loss in the first quarter was $109.3 million. Beam does recognize some revenue from licenses and collaboration, generating $7.47 million last quarter.

Despite the slow progress in the clinic, Beam’s future ultimately hinges on its proprietary base editing platform. The company hopes that its base editing technology “potentially enables a differentiated class of precision genetic medicines that target a single base in the genome without making a double-stranded break in the DNA."

Conclusion

To wrap up, Beam is leaner since my last look, but not all that much has changed. Its development pace remains frustratingly slow. These trials take a long time to establish safety and efficacy. When these products are approved, long-term data is necessary to convince patients to take the plunge into gene therapy. As such, regulatory approval is often the first step in a long journey for gene therapy developers.

Its decision to rid its pipeline of CAR-T is wise, but what remains are moonshots with low odds of market success. How many gene therapies do we need for SCD? Likewise, AATD has a crowded investigational pipeline. So, a bet on BEAM is a bet on its tech, and no one knows for sure if it will work. If it works, will its products sell?

As for a rating on BEAM, I remain cautious. However, I can see BEAM’s utility in a barbell portfolio for those interested in the tech and with the patience of a saint. This merits a rating upgrade to Hold, but this is more representative of a change in my investing philosophy over Beam’s own doing.

Notably, despite its stock slumping, Beam cannot be described as “cheap” with a $1.7 billion market capitalization. Yes, cash makes up a good portion of this, but it will likely be depleted pretty quickly, potentially leading to more shareholder dilution.

data.title: Carillon Reams Core Plus Bond Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

U.S. President Donald Trump unveils heavy tariffs in his first weeks.

The current administration appears willing to follow through on its campaign promises of punitive tariffs. This involved imposing and subsequently suspending tariffs, often in rapid succession, followed by additional adjustments. The opaque rationale behind these initial actions has contributed to heightened economic uncertainty. Tariffs targeting Canada, Mexico, and China were initially presented as measures to combat narcotics trafficking, address illegal immigration, and safeguard the U.S. manufacturing sector. However, their inconsistent application suggests a strategic intent that may deviate from the stated goals. Follow-on tariffs focusing on steel, aluminum, and the automotive industry, as well as those targeting China, appear more likely to persist. The outcome of these and future tariff actions remains to be seen.

Loss of confidence due to tariff uncertainty.

Inconsistent communication, uneven tariff implementation, and concern over long-term implications have generated substantial uncertainty for the business community, which can be seen in declining University of Michigan Surveys of Consumers data. In the short term, global markets have experienced significant disruptions, leading to the unwinding of the “Trump trade.” This phenomenon drove an equity rally, elevated interest rates, and strengthened the U.S. dollar following the election and through the early weeks of the administration. However, investor confidence began eroding by February, prompting a reversal of these trends. This shorter-term uncertainty may soon have adverse effects on long-term investment decisions.

Rapidly shifting foreign policy.

International policy decisions, particularly efforts to distance the United States from the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO), have been significant for the war in Ukraine and Europe’s broader security landscape. President Trump’s advocacy for an end to the conflict in Ukraine has inadvertently weakened Russia’s international isolation. The shift has had profound effects on global commodity markets, affecting energy prices most acutely. These developments prompted a swift response from Germany, which increased its infrastructure and defense spending beyond the limits established by E.U. regulations. Consequently, German interest rates rose sharply, which may elevate the global floor for real interest rates. The policy shift also strengthened the euro; Germany’s actions may offer a pathway to economic growth after two years of contraction.

Fed resurrects “transitory” description.

The decline in inflation has stalled, and the resurgence raises concerns about the effectiveness of efforts to bring it back to the Federal Reserve’s (Fed’s) 2% target. Rate cuts implemented in the final months of last year began to feel somewhat premature; inflation has become persistently elevated at a level that exceeds comfort. The introduction of tariffs is likely to drive up prices temporarily — the Fed has described it as “transitory,” and while the central bank has faced significant criticism for using that term in the past, it may be more appropriate in this instance. The Fed has acknowledged increased uncertainty in its ability to meet employment and inflation objectives, but markets continue to price in potential rate cuts this year. Investors are looking beyond the immediate inflationary impact of tariffs and focusing on their potentially longer-term economic drag.

Spread sectors were weaker on investor uncertainty.

Sector excess returns were negative in the first quarter; high-yield (HY) credit, investment grade (IG) credit, and asset-backed securities (ABS) all underperformed significantly. Commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) and mortgage-backed securities (MBS) had modestly negative excess returns. Most of the yield curve moved sharply lower in a largely parallel shift. Front-end maturities, defined as 3 months and shorter, did not move much. The Fed did not adjust rates during the quarter, but the back end of the curve responded to a weaker economic growth outlook. The curve is now inverted out to the 3-year point, but it has normalized beyond that. Future Fed actions have become increasingly uncertain.

Portfolio Review

Following a sharp move upward in the fourth quarter of 2024, the yield curve saw a sharp move downward in the first quarter of 2025. The move occurred in the longer end of the curve while the front end remained anchored, resulting in a relative flattening. The Fund’s longer duration positively impacted performance, which was slightly offset by its curve positioning.

Sector allocation and security selection contributed to performance during the quarter, primarily driven by emerging market/foreign exchange sector allocation. The underweight to investment grade (IG) corporate credit, on a cheapest-to-deliver basis, was a positive contributor as spreads widened throughout the quarter. The small overweight in asset-backed securities (ABS) detracted from performance as spreads increased. The overweight to MBS detracted as mortgages underperformed due to rising interest rate volatility. High yield (HY) corporate credit detracted as risk positions sold off during the quarter.

Security selection in mortgage-backed securities made a positive contribution as high-coupon mortgages outperformed lower-coupon securities. Selection within ABS also contributed; the portfolio’s focus on securities with attractive embedded options has continued to perform well. IG corporate selection was a more limited detractor, with utilities underperforming.

The allocation to agency residential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS) decreased, and exposure to non-agency RMBS also fell by a modest amount. Mortgage sector valuations were compelling at the start of the quarter, and they continue to represent a meaningful overweight. Exposure to IG corporates increased to a modest overweight relative to the index as corporate credit spreads reversed their trend and widened during the quarter. All three sectors (utilities, financials and industrials) of IG corporate allocations were overweight relative to the index, albeit slightly. The Fund established an initial toe-hold position in HY corporate credit during the quarter as spreads began to widen. ABS exposure increased during the quarter. The weight in commercial mortgage-backed securities increased, but it continues to represent a low absolute exposure. The exposure to non-U.S. dollar bonds increased slightly, although the allocation to non-U.S. dollar currencies was unchanged. The allocation to U.S. Treasurys increased, although it remains notably underweight relative to the index.

Portfolio duration fell during the quarter as the yield curve flattened and rates moved sharply lower. Duration ended the quarter slightly longer relative to the benchmark, reflecting the decline in real rates. Yield curve exposure at the end of the quarter featured overweights to the 2- to 5-year, 7- to 10-year, and 20- to 30-year duration segments. This was offset by underweights to the 5- to 7-year and 10- to 20-year duration segments.

Outlook

More tariff uncertainty.

A presidential administration’s first 100 days traditionally establishes the tone for its subsequent four years. However, the current administration has needed less than half that time. The emerging tone reflects a strategy of expansive action taken at a frenetic pace, designed to advance rapidly while keeping the opposition off balance. This administration also has a tendency to implement ideas without sufficient vetting beforehand. One notable example is in the realm of global trade, particularly tariffs. Any individual tariff may lack longevity, but the uncertainty surrounding their application is highly likely to persist. The uneven implementation of these tariffs has made their ultimate impact on global trade highly unpredictable.

DOGE initiative likely to hit resistance.

Despite considerable attention surrounding the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE), meaningful cost-cutting success may prove elusive. Legal challenges are expected to intensify, which could threaten the group’s initial momentum. The concept of an efficient government is widely appealing, but the rapid elimination of jobs — and in some cases, entire government entities — is unlikely to proceed without significant opposition, because political careers have been built on the ability to deliver tangible benefits to constituents.

The Fed’s job is now more difficult.

The Fed implemented a cumulative rate cut of 100 basis points in the final four months of 2024, aiming to normalize monetary policy after high single-digit inflation (stemming from post-COVID economic excesses) had necessitated historic rate hikes. The U.S. election and consequential shifts in global trade since then have significantly complicated the central bank’s mandate. Tariffs, a favorite tool of the new administration, exert upward pressures on prices that intensify inflationary challenges and limit the Fed’s ability to justify further rate reductions. The substantial tariffs introduced thus far could slow economic growth markedly, depending on their duration and scope. This challenge heightens the Fed’s imperative to consider rate cuts if the economy decelerates excessively. The politically charged environment adds to these difficulties; the Fed may face obstacles in preserving its historic independence, further complicating the role of its chair.

Geopolitical shift could spark some growth.

The evolving geopolitical landscape could redefine U.S. relationships with traditional allies, particularly with President Trump’s emphasis on increased defense contributions from other NATO members. This stance prompted a swift response from Germany, which circumvented E.U. spending constraints to bolster investments in infrastructure and defense. The durability of this shift remains uncertain, but it carries significant implications for Germany’s efforts to emerge from its recent economic stagnation. Should the broader European Union follow Germany’s lead and assume greater responsibility for defensive efforts, debt levels could become elevated and exert a lasting influence on the region’s growth trajectory.

Please consider the investment objectives, risks, charges, and expenses of any fund carefully before investing. Call 800.421.4814 or your financial professional for a prospectus, which contains this and other important information about the funds. Read the prospectus carefully before you invest or send money.

Risk Considerations:

The return of principal in a fixed income fund is not guaranteed. Fixed income funds have the same interest rate, inflation, issuer, maturity and credit risks that are associated with underlying fixed income securities owned by the fund. Mortgage- and Asset-Backed Securities are subject to prepayment risk and the risk of default on the underlying mortgages or other assets.

High-yield securities involve greater risk than investment grade securities and tend to be more sensitive to economic conditions and credit risk.

Foreign investments present additional risks due to currency fluctuations, economic and political factors, government regulations, differences in accounting standards and other factors. Investments in emerging markets involve even greater risks.

Derivatives such as options, futures contracts, currency forwards or swap agreements may involve greater risks than if the Fund invested in the referenced obligation directly. Derivatives are subject to risks such as market risk, liquidity risk, interest rate risk, credit risk, and management risk. Derivative investments could lose more than the principal amount invested. The Fund may use derivatives for hedging purposes or as part of its investment strategy. The use of leverage and derivatives investments could accelerate losses to the fund. These losses could exceed the amount originally invested.

The Fund may, at times, experience higher-than-average portfolio turnover, which may generate significant taxable gains and increased trading expenses, which, in turn, may lower the Fund’s return.

Past performance is not indicative of future results, and investing involves risk, including the risk of loss. All information as of March 31, 2025. Opinions expressed are the current opinions as of the date appearing in this material only. This material should not be construed as research or investment advice. No part of this material may, without Carillon Tower Advisers’ prior written consent, be copied, photocopied, or duplicated in any form, by any means.

The information provided should not be construed as a recommendation to buy, sell, or hold any particular security. The data is shown for informational purposes only and is not indicative of future portfolio characteristics or returns. Portfolio holdings are not stagnant and may change over time without prior notice.

Bond Ratings: Ratings are by Moody’s, S&P, and/or Fitch. Ratings provided by nationally recognized statistical rating organizations, also called ratings agencies, are appraisals of a particular issuer’s creditworthiness, including the possibility that the issuer will not be able to pay interest or repay principal. Ratings are not recommendations to buy, sell or hold a security, nor do ratings remove market risk. Securities with the same rating can actually trade at significantly different prices. In addition, ratings are subject to review, revision, suspension, reduction or withdrawal at any time, and a rating agency may place an issuer under review or credit watch. Additionally, Fitch reports are available for municipal bonds. More about ratings is available at moodys.com, standardandpoors.com, and fitchratings.com.

Definitions

Basis points (bps) are measurements used in discussions of interest rates and other percentages in finance. One basis point is equal to 1/100th of 1%, or 0.01%.

A credit spread is the difference in yield between a U.S. Treasury bond and another debt security with the same maturity but different credit quality. Also referred to as “bond spreads” or “default spreads,” credit spreads are measured in basis points, with a 1% difference in yield equaling a spread of 100 basis points. Credit spreads reflect the debt security’s risk compared with that of Treasury bond — which is considered to be risk-free — and they are considered to be “tighter” when the spread is smaller or “looser” when the spread is larger.

Duration incorporates a bond’s yield, coupon, final maturity and call features into one number, expressed in years, that indicates how price-sensitive a bond or portfolio is to changes in interest rates. Bonds with higher durations carry more risk and have higher price volatility than bonds with lower durations.

Cheapest-to-deliver terms, in U.S. Treasury bond futures contracts, allow any bonds to be delivered that are within certain maturity ranges and have certain coupon rates.

Headwind is a term used to describe events or market forces that hinder the prospects for performance in an individual investment or group of investments.

High-yield bonds have credit ratings below BBB- from Standard & Poor’s or below Baa3 from Moody’s.

Investment-grade refers to fixed-income securities rated BBB or better by Standard & Poor’s or Baa or better by Moody’s.

The North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO) is a political and military alliance of 32 countries from Europe and North America that is organized to guarantee their security and cooperation.

Overweight describes a portfolio position in an industry sector or some other category that is greater than the corresponding weight level in a benchmark portfolio.

Spread tightening refers to the contraction of credit spreads in response to changes in economic conditions that cause a decline in credit risk.

Spread sector is a term used in fixed income investing to describe nongovernmental fixed-in- come securities, such as corporate bonds or mortgage bonds, whether investment grade or high yield, that provide an additional yield, or “spread,” over the yield of a risk-free government bond.

A real interest rate is an interest rate that has been adjusted to remove the effects of inflation. Once adjusted, it reflects the real cost of funds to a borrower and the real yield to a lender or to an investor.

Overweight describes a portfolio position in an industry sector or some other category that is greater than the corresponding weight level in a benchmark portfolio.

Tailwind is a term used to describe events or market forces that exert a positive influence on an investment’s performance. The opposite of a tailwind is a headwind, which contributes to an investment’s underperformance.

The term “transitory” has been used by the U.S. Federal Reserve to describe inflation that is not expected to endure for extensive periods of time.

U.S. Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities (TIPS) provide protection against inflation. The principal of a TIPS instrument increases with inflation and decreases with deflation, as measured by the Consumer Price Index. When a TIPS matures, investors are paid the adjusted principal or original principal, whichever is greater.

Trump trade refers to the expectation that policies and actions of the Trump administration could benefit certain areas of the capital markets.

Underweight describes a portfolio position in an industry sector or some other category that is less than the corresponding weight level in a benchmark portfolio.

University of Michigan Surveys of Consumers conduct monthly telephone surveys asking consumers in the continental United States about what they think now and what their expectations are for their personal finances, business conditions, and buying conditions in the future. Their responses are used to calculate monthly measures of consumer sentiment that can be compared to a base value of 100 set in 1966.

A yield curve is a line that plots yields (interest rates) of bonds having equal credit quality but differing maturity dates. The slope of the yield curve gives an idea of future interest rate changes and economic activity. Investors and market analysts watch certain yield curves for signs of inversion, when yields for longer-term debt instruments fall below yields on short-term debt with the same credit quality. Inversions are watched as potential signs of a weakening economy and in certain cases, a harbinger of recessions.

Benchmark Index

The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index is composed of the total U.S. investment-grade bond market. The market-weighted index includes Treasuries, agencies, CMBS, ABS, and investment-grade corporates. It is not possible to invest in an index.

“Bloomberg®” and the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index are service marks of Bloomberg Finance L.P. and its affiliates, including Bloomberg Index Services Limited (“BISL”), the administrator of the indices (collectively, “Bloomberg”) and have been licensed for use for certain purposes by Carillon Tower Advisers and Reams Asset Management. Bloomberg is not affiliated with Carillon Tower Advisers or Reams Asset Management, and Bloomberg does not approve, endorse, review, or recommend the Carillon Reams Core Bond Fund. Bloomberg does not guarantee the timeliness, accurateness, or completeness of any data or information relating to the Carillon Reams Core Bond Fund.

Carillon Tower Advisers is the investment adviser for the Carillon Family of Funds and Scout Investments is the sub-adviser to the Carillon Reams Core Bond Fund. Reams Asset Management is a division of Scout Investments. Scout Investments is a wholly owned subsidiary of Carillon Tower Advisers.
